# ClashControl

> Free, open-source online IFC viewer and BIM clash detection that runs in the browser. View IFC building models in 3D, detect hard and soft clashes between disciplines, run IDS and data-quality checks, and export to BCF 2.1. A free alternative to Solibri and Navisworks. No install, no license, no subscription.

ClashControl is a single-page web app at https://www.clashcontrol.io/ aimed at AEC / BIM coordinators. It is both an online IFC viewer and a clash-detection / model-checking tool. The full app — viewer, clash engine, issues panel, BCF export, IDS validation, data quality checks — runs entirely client-side. Optional addons (Revit live link, local exact-mesh engine, smart bridge for LLMs) connect to localhost services. It is commonly used as a free alternative to Solibri, Navisworks and BIMcollab for the everyday IFC coordination loop.

## Core capabilities

- Online IFC viewer: IFC 2x3 and IFC4 via web-ifc WASM, 3D orbit/pan/zoom, model tree by storey/type/discipline/material
- Mesh-based clash detection: AABB broad-phase + BVH tri-tri narrow-phase (Möller–Trumbore), optional WASM acceleration
- Hard clashes (geometric intersection), soft clashes (clearance/tolerance), and **visibility clashes (sight-line obstruction + inverse coverage)** — the third category nobody else ships. Ray-cast against the same BVH the clash engine builds, with viewer/target/obstructer filters by IFC type + name regex + manual pick, and 3×3 target-surface sampling so partial occlusion grades as "low" priority while full blockage grades as "normal". Inverse coverage mode flips the question: "every viewer must be visible from at least one target" — unlocks surveillance camera gaps, smoke detector reach, patient-bay observability. Regulation preset library: exit signs (generic + EN 1838 + BS 5499), workplace window views (NL Arbo), nurse-station LOS, wheelchair eye-height variants, reception-front-door LOS, surveillance / smoke / patient-bay coverage. ⌘K, NL chat ("visibility exit signs", "coverage gaps cameras"), or the Standards panel — all routes the same engine.
- **As-built verification: point cloud ↔ IFC manual three-point rigid alignment** — pick three reference points on the design, three on the scan; CC computes the rigid transform in closed form (orthonormal-frame matching, no SVD, no ICP iterations) and the scan lands in the design coordinate frame. Persists across reloads. The foundational workflow for comparing reality against intent.
- BCF 2.1 / 3.0 import and export (viewpoints, markup, snapshots, custom AI-provenance + Revit-ID round-trip labels)
- 3D viewer with section planes, section box, floor cuts (draggable horizontal handle), walk mode, render styles, measurement tools
- IDS (Information Delivery Specification) validation
- Data quality checks: BIM basics, ILS, NL-SfB classification
- Accessibility (toegankelijkheid) geometric checks
- Issue management with PDF overlay and saved viewpoints
- AI assistant: plain-language commands, clash titles and triage; bring-your-own LLM via the one-click Connector (Ollama, LM Studio, llama.cpp, Jan, or Claude Desktop)
- Real-world basemap placement and LAS / PLY / PCD / XYZ / PTS / PTX point cloud overlay (Leica PTS + PTX scan formats first-class)
- OpenAEC Foundation bridge: discovers a running sibling app (open-pointcloud-studio) on localhost port 49100+ and syncs cameras, opens files, round-trips viewpoints over their HTTP `/eval` endpoint
- Shared projects via short keys (no login)
- Progressive Web App — installable, offline-capable

## As-built verification workflow

ClashControl is the BIM coordination tool that ships **point cloud ↔ IFC manual rigid alignment** as a first-class feature. The flow:

1. Load the design IFC and a laser scan (LAS / PLY / PCD / XYZ / PTS / PTX).
2. Enter alignment mode via `_ccAlignStart(targetId)` or the panel button.
3. Click three reference points on the IFC, then their three matching points on the scan — easy to find via column corners, slab edges, prominent fittings.
4. CC computes the rigid transform in closed form: orthonormal-frame matching where the basis of each triangle (`ex = (B−A) normalised`, `en = ((B−A) × (C−A)) normalised`, `ey = en × ex`) defines a rotation `R = M_dst · M_src^T` and translation `t = dst_centroid − R · src_centroid`. No SVD, no iterative ICP, no degenerate-case branching beyond "three picks must be non-collinear".
5. The scan lands in the design coordinate frame. Worst-pick residual surfaces as a sanity check. Alignment persists in `localStorage` keyed by scan file name, so a reload restores it automatically.
6. Optional: click **Refine (ICP)** to iteratively snap the scan to the nearest IFC elements. Each iteration samples ~2000 scan points, finds the closest IFC element centroid per sample, picks the three cleanest non-collinear correspondences, and re-runs the same closed-form 3-pair solver. SVD-free, ~20 iterations to convergence, dependency-free.
7. Optional: click **Deviation map** to colour the cloud green→amber→red by distance to the nearest IFC element. Spatial-grid acceleration with time-sliced batches keeps the UI responsive on multi-million-point scans.
8. Optional: click **Issues at hotspots** to auto-create one BCF-compatible issue per spatial cluster of red points above a configurable size. Each issue gets a 3D snapshot captured automatically (camera positioned looking at the cluster centroid before render), linked via `viewpoint.linkedId`. Turns survey deviation into a coordination backlog instantly.
9. Optional: click **Report PDF** to open a print-ready deviation report — cover page with model + scan names, summary statistics, alignment + deviation cards, hotspot index table, and one page per hotspot with the captured snapshot, location, priority, status, and description. User prints / saves as PDF.

Public API (stable, MIT): `window._ccAlignStart(targetId)`, `_ccAlignCancel()`, `_ccAlignClear(targetId)`, `_ccAlignStatus()`, `_ccAlignOnChange(cb)`, `_ccAlignRefineICP(targetId, opts)`, `_ccDeviationCompute(targetId, opts)`, `_ccDeviationClear(targetId)`, `_ccDeviationCreateIssues(targetId, opts)`, `_ccDeviationExportReport(targetId)`.

## OpenAEC Foundation interop

ClashControl integrates with sibling OpenAEC Foundation desktop apps over localhost. Phase 1 targets [open-pointcloud-studio](https://github.com/OpenAEC-Foundation/open-pointcloud-studio): the addon discovers a running instance on port 49100+, shipping actions via their HTTP `/eval` endpoint. Public API: `_ccOpenAECStatus()`, `_ccOpenAECSyncCamera()`, `_ccOpenAECPullCamera()`, `_ccOpenAECOpenCloud(path)`, `_ccOpenAECListClouds()`, `_ccOpenAECReconnect()`.

## BCF point-cloud reference extension (`cc:scanRef`)

ClashControl's BCF 2.1 / 3.0 export embeds point-cloud references inside the standard `<DocumentReferences>` element of each Topic's `markup.bcf`. The payload sits in the `<Description>` text with a `cc:scanRef=` prefix and is a JSON object:

```
<DocumentReference>
  <ReferencedDocument>scans/site-20260609.las</ReferencedDocument>
  <Description>cc:scanRef={"schema":1,"kind":"pointcloud","file":"scans/site-20260609.las","alignment":{"applied":[...16 floats column-major...],"timestamp":"2026-06-09T14:23:11.000Z","worstResidual":0.018},"deviation":{"min":0.001,"max":0.182,"mean":0.034,"greenAt":0.02,"redAt":0.1}}</Description>
</DocumentReference>
```

Fields:
- `schema` — extension schema version (currently 1).
- `kind` — always `"pointcloud"` in this version.
- `file` — relative path to the scan; both apps resolve against the folder the BCF was opened from. The scan is NOT embedded in the BCF zip (scans are routinely hundreds of MB to several GB).
- `alignment.applied` — the 4×4 rigid transform that maps the scan into the design IFC coordinate frame, as 16 floats in column-major order (Three.js Matrix4 element order).
- `alignment.worstResidual` — metres; the largest pick-pair distance after the transform was applied. Sanity check.
- `deviation` — optional, present only if the deviation heatmap was computed. Distance statistics in metres plus the green/red thresholds used.

Other BCF readers preserve unknown DocumentReference Descriptions, so the data round-trips cleanly without breaking compatibility. The reciprocal contract for sibling tools: on import, look for any DocumentReference whose Description starts with `cc:scanRef=`, JSON-parse the remainder, and apply the transform. CC's importer reconstitutes the reference list onto the issue as `issue.scanReferences = [...]`.

OpenAEC Foundation sibling apps are invited to recognise the same prefix. The extension is MIT and documented here as the canonical reference.

## BCF visibility-clash extension (`cc:vis*` labels)

ClashControl's third clash category — visibility (sight-line obstruction) — round-trips through BCF 2.1 / 3.0 via standard `<Labels>` elements on the Topic. Standard BCF readers preserve unknown Labels, so the data round-trips cleanly without breaking compatibility, and sister tools that implement this convention can render full visibility semantics instead of degrading to a generic Request.

Label keys (each appears in its own `<Labels>` element):

| Label                  | Meaning                                                  |
|------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| `cc:visType=visibility`| Marks the topic as a sight-line obstruction clash.       |
| `cc:visFrom=x,y,z`     | World-space viewer pose (sight-line origin), metres.     |
| `cc:visTo=x,y,z`       | World-space target sample point (sight-line endpoint).   |
| `cc:visBlocker=<id>`   | ExpressId (or stable identifier) of the obstructer.      |
| `cc:visBlockerName=…`  | Human-readable name of the obstructer.                   |
| `cc:visBlockerType=…`  | IFC type of the obstructer (e.g. `IfcColumn`).           |
| `cc:visDist=<m>`       | Distance from viewer to blocker hit point, metres.       |
| `cc:visRange=<m>`      | Total intended sight distance (viewer → target), metres. |
| `cc:visPartial=1`      | Partial occlusion (some samples clear, some blocked).    |
| `cc:visBlockedSamples=N` | Number of target surface samples that were blocked.    |
| `cc:visTotalSamples=N` | Total target surface samples tested (3×3 grid by default).|
| `cc:visOcclusionRate=R`| Fraction blocked, 0..1 (handy for sort/triage).          |
| `cc:visCoverageGap=1`  | Inverse-mode result: viewer point not visible from any target. |

On import, CC reconstructs the clash with `type: 'visibility'`, attaches `sightFrom` / `sightTo` / `elemBlocker` to the issue payload, and the 3D viewer re-renders the sight line (solid green to blocker, dashed red to target) the moment the issue becomes active.

Sibling implementers: this is a minimal, MIT-documented schema. We're happy to coordinate on extensions — sight-cone metadata, multi-sample partial occlusion, regulation-clause references.

## Agentic governance (audit trail)

Every AI-touched decision in ClashControl carries provenance: which model produced it, when, and which endpoint it came from. Surfaced as an "AI" chip in the Issues panel (hover for the model + timestamp); persisted on clashes and triage results as `aiProvenance: {actor, model, source, at}`. This addresses the gap Martyn Day called out in AEC Magazine ("Agentic BIM's missing infrastructure", April 2026): the current generation of agentic BIM platforms treat MCP as a governance layer when it is only an interface layer. ClashControl logs every AI decision with model attribution, runs an explicit graduated-autonomy envelope (the built-in assistant is intentionally basic; clash-resolution escalates to the user's own LLM), and uses no shared foundation-model monoculture — your LLM is your choice.

## Roadmap

- **As-built verification Phase 3** — triangle-accurate deviation distance (currently AABB-distance for performance; triangle-distance for QA-grade reports), per-issue scan-anchored viewpoint snapshots, and a deviation report PDF export. Builds on the alignment + ICP + heatmap + auto-issue stack that's already shipping.
- **BCF point-cloud references** — extend BCF 2.1 / 3.0 export with `DocumentReference` pointing at the scan that was active when the viewpoint was captured, plus the alignment transform. CC + OpenAEC sibling apps can round-trip the full design + as-built composite in one BCF zip.
- **Tauri desktop build** — wrap the existing single-file viewer in a Tauri shell for Windows / macOS / Linux installers. Larger heap, native file-system access, system-tray + file-association registration. Phase 2 (later, customer-driven): port hot paths to Rust.
- **Gaussian Splat as-built overlay (Phase 1 shipping)** — drag-drop .splat / .ksplat / .spz files to load drone-capture splats as a reference layer rendered on a sibling canvas behind the IFC model. Phase 2 adds 3D Tiles streaming + Esri Site Scan tileset URLs.
- WebGPU-accelerated clash detection — scheduled when a customer hits unacceptable detection times on a large federation. Expected ~10× speedup on 10k+ element models.

- Free IFC data quality checker (0–100 score): https://www.clashcontrol.io/data-quality-checker-online/
- Developer API & plugin docs: https://www.clashcontrol.io/developers/

## Public APIs

ClashControl exposes four extension surfaces: (1) JavaScript globals on `window.ClashControl.*` / `window._cc*` for reading state, dispatching actions, accessing the viewport camera + bounds, running quality and accessibility checks, and loading reference layers (splats, point clouds); (2) an addon system at `addons/*.js` where any IIFE can expose its own globals and register via `_ccRegisterAddon`; (3) a localhost REST API on `127.0.0.1:19803` via the Smart Bridge binary, with auto-generated OpenAPI; (4) an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server exposing 51 tools so Claude Desktop, ChatGPT Connectors, or any function-calling LLM can drive the viewer. All MIT-licensed. Full reference at https://www.clashcontrol.io/developers/.

## Privacy & data security

ClashControl processes IFC, GLB, and BCF files entirely on the user's device via WebAssembly (web-ifc). Files never leave the browser; there is no server-side model upload. Optional features that touch our backend (anonymous usage analytics, shared-project issue metadata sync, AI assistant) are individually opt-out and never send model geometry. The entire source is open under MIT at github.com/clashcontrol-io/ClashControl and the app is a single static HTML file that can be self-hosted on a corporate intranet. Full details at https://www.clashcontrol.io/security/.

## How it compares

- vs Navisworks: ClashControl is free, browser-based, BCF-first; Navisworks is a paid Autodesk desktop product. ClashControl targets the daily IFC coordination loop, not every Navisworks workflow.
- vs Solibri: both speak IFC + BCF; ClashControl is free and zero-install, Solibri has deeper rule scripting.
- vs BIMcollab ZOOM: similar browser-based approach; ClashControl is open-source and runs without an account.
